discovery.text: From Allan B. Jacobs' Great Streets (1993). The great promenade. Jacobs' opening case study — a living room for the city, not a road.

narrative.summary: The great promenade. Jacobs' opening case study — a living room for the city, not a road.

blocks.title: Why Jacobs Chose It
blocks.body: Wide central pedestrian median, cafés and kiosks spilling into the walk, plane trees filtering light, human scale on every side. The classic example of a street where the architecture serves the life below, not the other way around.

blocks.title: How to Walk It
blocks.body: Walk the full length. Jacobs is explicit: you have to walk a great street to understand it — the sequence, the proportions, how the edges change. Give it an hour minimum. Sit at a café or on a bench midway. Notice what makes it feel alive.

blocks.name: Bar Quiosc Modern
blocks.why: One of the original kiosk bars built into the median promenade itself — the edge condition Jacobs admires most. Sit here and you ARE La Rambla.

blocks.name: Bar Boqueria
blocks.address: Mercat de la Boqueria, La Rambla 91, Barcelona
blocks.why: Inside La Boqueria market — the great market hall that anchors the most famous section of La Rambla. The covered market as edge institution, drawing street life inward.

blocks.name: Mític Bar
blocks.address: La Rambla 91, Mercat de la Boqueria, Barcelona
blocks.why: Another market bar at the Boqueria stalls. The ritual of eating at the market counter while the promenade flows outside is one of Barcelona's defining urban pleasures.
